title: Kudla's Modularity Conjecture
speaker: Salim Tayou
abstract: Kudla and Millson proved in the 80's that the generating series of special cycles in orthogonal and unitary Shimura varieties are modular forms and a well-established conjecture of Kudla asks about extensions to toroidal compactifications. In this talk, I will answer Kudla's conjecture for divisors in O(p,2) and cycles in U(n,1) up to the middle degree, in cohomology. Joint work with François Greer and Philip Engel.

proof_stages.label: Unitary Shimura variety and special cycles
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a Hermitian O_K-lattice of signature (n+1,1), forms the unitary Shimura variety Gamma\D, recalls its moduli interpretation, and defines special cycles Z(M) by imposing orthogonality to tuples of lattice vectors with fixed Hermitian Gram matrix.

proof_stages.label: Kudla-Millson series and compactified modularity problem
proof_stages.summary: The generating series of special-cycle classes is defined, Kudla-Millson's cohomological modularity theorem is recalled, and Kudla's conjectural Chow and compactification refinements are stated together with the announced theorem proving the boundary-corrected statement for the canonical toroidal compactification up to the middle range.

proof_stages.label: Boundary geometry and homological reduction
proof_stages.summary: The Baily-Borel boundary is described as finitely many cusps indexed by primitive isotropic rank-one submodules, the toroidal boundary divisors B_I are identified as CM abelian varieties E tensor (I^perp/I), and an ample-conormal Lefschetz-type lemma reduces testing modularity to pairings with cycles supported on the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Restriction of special cycles to boundary divisors
proof_stages.summary: For each cusp I the quotient J=I^perp/I is positive definite, and the intersection of Z(M) with B_I is expressed as a sum of explicit kernel-type cycles indexed by tuples in J with Gram matrix M, while non-positive-definite terms vanish on the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Poincare-dual polynomial forms and boundary theta series
proof_stages.summary: Explicit polynomial families of harmonic differential forms on B_I are constructed whose cohomology classes are Poincare dual to the boundary special cycles, so pairing with a boundary homology class becomes a theta series attached to a polynomial in a finite-dimensional space F_{n,g}.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-Freitag theta theorem and quasi-modularity
proof_stages.summary: The relevant theta series is analyzed using the theorem of Freitag in the orthogonal case and Shimura in the unitary case: applying exp(-Delta/8pi) to the polynomial produces a non-holomorphic modular form, whose holomorphic part is the original series, yielding quasi-modularity and genuine modularity in the harmonic case.

proof_stages.label: Lefschetz-SL2 decomposition and boundary correction
proof_stages.summary: The pairing map from boundary cohomology to polynomial spaces is shown to be an SL2-isomorphism compatible with Lefschetz decomposition, allowing the non-harmonic raised pieces of the boundary restriction to be subtracted explicitly from Z(M) so that the corrected generating series is holomorphic modular.

examples.name: Siegel upper half-space analogy
examples.note: The Hermitian upper half-space used for the variable tau is compared with the more familiar Siegel space only to make the modular-form variable concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The Siegel upper half-space is invoked only as a familiar analogy for the Hermitian upper half-space, with no work carried out on the Siegel case itself.

passes.reason: Siegel upper half-space is a classical Hermitian symmetric domain used only as an analogy, not as an obstruction.

examples.name: Free boundary lattice gives a product of CM elliptic curves
examples.note: When I^perp/I is free of rank n over O_K, the boundary abelian variety B_I becomes E^n, illustrating the tensor construction of the toroidal boundary component.
examples.engagement_reason: The boundary component built from the CM elliptic curve tensor the boundary lattice is analyzed: the speaker identifies the free case with a product of CM elliptic curves and then defines pairings, kernels, boundary special cycles, Poincaré dual forms, and theta series on it.

passes.reason: The free rank-n boundary lattice gives the split product E^n of CM elliptic curves as an illustrative boundary component, with no sporadic, limiting, or extremal role.
In the free case the boundary abelian variety becomes the highly regular self-product E^n of a CM elliptic curve, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
The free boundary component is a limiting stratum and in this case the highly regular split CM self-product E^n.
The free quotient case yields the highly regular isotypic product E^n of a CM elliptic curve, used as a simple boundary-component description rather than to refute anything.
In the free case the boundary component is the regular self-product E^n of a CM elliptic curve lying as a compactification boundary stratum.
It is a limiting boundary stratum, and in the free case has the highly regular product form E^n of identical CM elliptic curves.
The free boundary component is a limiting boundary divisor, and in the free case it is the highly regular CM product E^n.
It illustrates a cusp/boundary component, and in the free case the object is the very regular product E^n of identical CM elliptic curves.
The free-module case is an illustrative boundary component isomorphic to a systematic product of CM elliptic curves, not an obstruction or sporadic/extreme case.
Used as a simplifying boundary-component case, where a free lattice makes the abelian variety the regular self-product E^n of a CM elliptic curve.
In the free case the boundary component is the regular product E^n of a CM elliptic curve, used descriptively rather than adversarially.
Used as an illustrative free-module case, giving the regular product abelian variety E^n with repeated CM elliptic factors.

examples.name: Orthogonal quadratic-lattice analogue
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes the parallel orthogonal story involving an elliptic curve, a quadratic lattice, and Kuga-Sato-type varieties, as an analogy for the boundary theta construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The orthogonal quadratic-lattice version is only discussed as a parallel story or work in progress, with no concrete computation or construction run through for that analogue.

passes.reason: The orthogonal quadratic-lattice version is a systematic parallel family rather than a special one-off or adversarial example.
